Politics & Power 🌴 Caribbean

Haiti Crisis: Gang Violence Tracker & Briefing (2026)

The briefing reports that armed gangs control 85–90% of Port-au-Prince, with 1.5 million internally displaced and a near-total state collapse. A UN-backed Gang Suppression Force is slowly deploying, but the humanitarian crisis deepens. The coverage relies on conflict tracking datasets and military updates.

Haitians are presented almost entirely through displacement figures and territorial percentages, reducing their suffering to abstract data points.

International security contractors and the Kenyan-led mission's leadership.

Towards a decolonial caribbean reparations movement

The article advocates for a decolonial approach to Caribbean reparations, linking present-day inequality to colonial exploitation. It calls for collective action to address structural injustices rooted in slavery and colonialism.

Portrayed as organizing for systemic change, Black Caribbean communities are depicted asserting demands for reparations rooted in colonial history and ongoing inequality.

European governments and former colonial powers.

Economy 🌴 Caribbean

Beer in the Caribbean — Grokipedia

The Grokipedia page on Caribbean beer presents a factual overview of brewing in the region without addressing the historical or ongoing economic exploitation of Black communities by multinational alcohol companies. It omits how colonial-era sugar plantations laid the groundwork for modern beer production, while contemporary marketing disproportionately targets Black consumers.

The encyclopedia entry treats Caribbean beer as a neutral commodity, erasing the region's history of colonial plantation economies and corporate extraction of local resources.

Global beverage corporations like Anheuser-Busch InBev and Heineken.

colonial legacycorporate extraction
Collected: 2026-07-20 20:01 Read original →

Decolonizing the Whitewashed World of Caribbean Rum

The article examines how the Caribbean rum industry remains dominated by white men, despite its African and Indigenous roots. It highlights ongoing colonial dynamics in ownership, marketing, and representation.

Black Caribbean people are portrayed as having their cultural industry stolen and whitewashed by colonial power structures that persist today.

White male executives and corporations dominating the rum industry.

Caribbean Rum Tourism Explosion Is Turning Distilleries and Culture Into Must-Visit Global Travel Experiences: Read to Experience More!

The article promotes Caribbean rum tourism as a cultural and economic boon, framing distilleries as authentic travel experiences. It ignores how this industry profits from colonial-era plantation systems and the ongoing exploitation of Black labor and land.

Policing 🌴 Caribbean

CBP seizes $53,800 in undeclared cash from traveler headed to Jamaica

A U.S. Customs and Border Protection officer seized over $53,800 in undeclared cash from a traveler flying from Fort Lauderdale to Kingston, Jamaica. The traveler was arrested and charged with money laundering, with officials highlighting efforts to combat bulk cash smuggling.

By singling out a Black traveler heading to Jamaica, the coverage implicitly links Black mobility with illicit financial activity and criminal intent.

U.S. Customs and Border Protection benefits from asset seizure and enforcement funding.

Policing 🌴 Caribbean

Off-duty cop murdered in bar, suspect held seeking treatment at hospital

An off-duty police officer, Dale Mayers, was killed during an attempted robbery at a bar in Trinidad. The suspect denied involvement, claiming he was shot in an unrelated incident, and is now hospitalized under guard.

The coverage reduces a Black police officer and his suspected killer to dehumanized figures in a crime report, implying Black lives are merely data points in a cycle of violence.

Policing 🌴 Caribbean

Chronic Law charged in US over alleged illegal firearm possession

Jamaican dancehall artist Chronic Law was arrested in the U.S. for illegal firearm possession after a high-speed chase. He faces up to 15 years in prison, with prosecutors citing alleged gang affiliation and flight risk.

Readers meet this Jamaican artist through police allegations of gang ties and weapon possession, reinforcing stereotypes linking Black entertainers to criminality.

U.S. law enforcement and the prison-industrial complex.

Sport 🌴 Caribbean

Barbados cricket legend Sir Garfield Sobers dies at 89

Sir Garfield Sobers, the legendary Barbados and West Indies cricketer, has died at 89 at his home in Barbados. His son shared details of his peaceful passing after a long illness, and the story honors his historic sporting legacy and status as a national hero.

The coverage celebrates Sir Garfield Sobers as a beloved national hero and cricket legend, highlighting his achievements, family bonds, and peaceful passing without invoking racial or structural critiques.

The West Indies Cricket Board and Barbados tourism industry.

Migration 🌴 Caribbean

Two Jamaicans deported by US to Eswatini decline government's offer to return home

Two Jamaican men deported by the US to Eswatini have refused Jamaica's offer to return home, instead hoping to rejoin their families in the US. The US has not explained why the men were sent to the southern African kingdom rather than Jamaica, raising concerns over due process and consular rights.

The men appear as victims of opaque US deportation policy, stranded in a foreign land, their past and agency obscured by bureaucratic and legal limbo.

US immigration enforcement agencies benefit from reduced legal obligations and costs.

Jamaican man charged with murdering niece in New York amid reported family inheritance dispute

A Jamaican man, Michael Foster, is charged with murdering his niece Julia Anderson in the Bronx, reportedly over an inheritance dispute. The victim was a hardworking woman who supported people with disabilities, while the suspect remains in custody.

The coverage centers on a Black immigrant as a perpetrator of violence, implicitly linking criminality to Caribbean identity and family conflict.

World 🌴 Caribbean

NewsJamaica records increase in child abuse reports as thousands of cases referred for investigation

Jamaica recorded over 14,600 child abuse reports in 2025, a 5% increase from 2024, with nearly 31,600 incidents. The most common concerns were behavioral problems, neglect, and cases needing care and protection, highlighting ongoing challenges in the child protection system.

The story reduces Jamaican children to data points, implying their suffering is a bureaucratic problem rather than a human crisis requiring systemic change.

Government agencies maintaining investigatory systems without meaningful prevention efforts.

Politics & Power 🌴 Caribbean

Haiti targets late-2026 elections despite gang violence, humanitarian crisis

Haiti plans elections by late 2026 amid gang violence and a severe humanitarian crisis, with over half the population needing aid. The UN warns that credible elections depend on security improvements, while international funding for relief remains insufficient.

Reducing Haitians to numbers—half needing aid, millions displaced—the coverage sidelines their ongoing resistance and complexity, framing them as a passive humanitarian problem.

International creditors and foreign corporations benefiting from Haiti's instability and exploitation.

Guyana confirms two dead, dozens missing after MV Barima ferry capsizes

A ferry capsized in Guyana, killing two and leaving dozens missing, with authorities blaming an inaccurate passenger manifest and crew members testing positive for drugs. The government has launched an investigation into negligence and possible criminal wrongdoing.

The story reduces Black Guyanese lives to missing bodies and a compromised manifest, implying their value is secondary to administrative failures.

The ferry operator and corrupt officials who profited from unregistered passengers.

Death toll rises as more bodies found following boating tragedy

A ferry capsized off Guyana's coast, killing at least 10 people with 70 still missing, as the government defends the vessel's seaworthiness. The captain and engineer tested positive for narcotics, but officials downplay safety concerns. The tragedy highlights inadequate infrastructure and regulatory failures in a historically marginalized region.

Trump says US strikes hit Iran in ‘honour’ of American soldiers killed

Economy 🌴 Caribbean

Fees fuel bank profits

Commercial banks in Barbados made significant profits from fees and deposit service charges, far exceeding interest paid to depositors. Over the past decade, depositors transferred $280.7 million to banks, reflecting a system that profits from fees and low savings interest.

Barbadians appear as passive income sources for banks, with the story emphasizing systemic extraction through fee structures and low interest rates.

Commercial banks in Barbados

Health 🌴 Caribbean

BAMP: Take that back, Santia

The Barbados Association of Medical Practitioners demands Deputy Prime Minister Santia Bradshaw retract her claim of a "medical mafia" influencing transplant access at Queen Elizabeth Hospital. Bradshaw argued that patients who pay privately can bypass long public waiting lists, a practice she fears the new transplant law could perpetuate.

Black Barbadians are portrayed as patients navigating a two-tier system where wealth can determine access to timely surgery, raising concerns about fairness.

Caribbean agri-firms want buyers and freight—not more workshops

Caribbean small agricultural businesses struggle with high freight costs, unreliable shipping, and weak distribution links, hindering their access to regional and international markets. Despite demand for their products, they face systemic barriers that training alone cannot solve.

Calls For Suriname to Follow Barbados and Trinidad and Tobago in Decolonization Process

Armand Zunder, chair of Suriname's National Reparations Commission, urges Suriname to follow Barbados and Trinidad and Tobago in decolonizing, citing the need to replace colonial symbols like the coat of arms and mountain names. He highlights how colonial imagery persists 50 years after independence.

The people of Suriname are shown as actively pushing for decolonization, rejecting inherited colonial symbols and demanding structural change.

Another foreign intervention will not solve the crisis in Haiti

Education 🌴 Caribbean EXPLICIT RACISM

Examining the Legacies of the Colonial Racialised Class Formation in the Education System of Jamaica

This article analyzes how Jamaica's education system perpetuates colonial racial class hierarchies, leaving rural Afro-Jamaican children in under-resourced primary schools while elite colonial-era high schools remain inaccessible. The author argues that neoliberal policies undermine reform efforts and calls for reparative justice to create an empowering educational framework.

Afro-Jamaican children from rural poor communities are depicted as victims of a colonial education system that systematically reproduces racialized class inequality.

The Jamaican elite and neoliberal economic forces benefit from this tiered schooling system.

Policing 🌴 Caribbean

Dodds inmate awarded $20 000

A High Court in Barbados ruled that prison officials violated the constitutional rights of inmate Corey Lynch by failing to provide adequate colostomy bags, causing him severe pain and humiliation. The court found systemic neglect, with Lynch's mother forced to buy supplies and a fellow inmate assisting with hygiene. Damages of $20,000 were awarded for inhuman and degrading treatment.

Lynch is portrayed as a medically vulnerable individual whose dignity was systematically violated by state neglect, revealing how Black bodies are devalued in carceral systems.

The Barbados prison system and state bureaucracy.

Barbados, Antigua & Barbuda advance to second round of AmeriCup

Barbados and Antigua and Barbuda won their groups in the FIBA AmeriCup 2029 Caribbean Pre-Qualifiers, advancing to the second round. Barbados went undefeated at 4-0, and Antigua and Barbuda finished 3-1, with standout individual performances including Kyrone Alexander's 32 points for Barbados.

Black Caribbean athletes are celebrated here as skilled competitors achieving regional success, which counters deficit narratives and highlights agency and collective pride.

Central Bank urges financial institutions to address BiMPay issues

Jamaica signs deal to accept non-national migrants deported from US

Jamaica has signed a non-binding memorandum of understanding to accept up to 10,000 non-national migrants deported from the US, as part of President Trump's mass deportation drive. Several Caribbean nations, including Antigua and Barbuda, Dominica, and Saint Lucia, have entered similar agreements under US visa pressure. Critics see the arrangement as an exploitative shift of migration responsibility onto smaller, less powerful nations.

The story presents Guyana as a sovereign nation navigating geopolitical tensions, with Black Guyanese citizens implicitly benefiting from diplomatic support and legal recourse.

France benefits by extending regional influence through French Guiana.

The UN reports nearly 1.5 million displaced in Haiti due to rampant armed violence, with most hosted in vulnerable communities. The humanitarian response remains severely underfunded, while political instability deepens.

Judge clarifies law on public protests: 'No police permission necessary'

A Trinidadian High Court judge ruled that public protests do not require police permission, clarifying a legal distinction between protests and marches. The case involved a businessman who was unlawfully instructed to stop his one-man protest against the government's safe-zone policy.

The ruling highlights a citizen's legal standing against police overreach, portraying the protester as an individual exercising fundamental rights amid state power.

The state and police benefit from maintained authority over protest limitations.

Outrage over police killing - CCTV shows victim with hands in air

CCTV footage contradicts police claims that Joshua Samaroo and Kaia Sealy were challenging officers; it shows Samaroo with hands up before being shot 17 times. Public outrage has grown, and the Police Complaints Authority is investigating while the police commissioner's initial account is discredited.

Black Caribbean people appear here as victims of excessive police force, with the framing casting doubt on official accounts and highlighting systemic violence.

North Carolina man sentenced for selling elderly Americans’ data to Jamaican scammers

A North Carolina man was sentenced to over 10 years for selling personal data of millions of elderly Americans to Jamaican scammers, who used it in lottery fraud causing $9.5 million in losses. The scheme operated from 2016 to 2023, with the defendant profiting over $5.2 million. Jamaican scammers are highlighted as the primary perpetrators.

Jamaican scammers are portrayed as predatory outsiders exploiting elderly Americans, reinforcing a stereotype linking Black Caribbean people to international fraud.

She criticizes politicians who ban patois in Parliament while using it to campaign, and urges younger Jamaicans to value traditional cultural forms like Hill & Gully.

Loving mother describes daughter’s struggle with Methylmalonic Acidemia

Theresa Small's daughter Thalisa, born with a rare metabolic disorder, was denied vital medication by Queen Elizabeth Hospital in Barbados. After relocating to the US, Thalisa graduated from culinary school, showcasing personal success despite ongoing health challenges.

The story portrays a Black mother and daughter as resilient individuals overcoming systemic barriers, highlighting personal triumph over medical neglect and institutional indifference.

A High Court judge ruled that the Trinidad and Tobago Coast Guard unlawfully delayed deciding on O'Brian Lightbourne's promotion complaint for over a year, ordering a decision within seven days. Westmoreland attorneys demand Gov’t prioritise restoration of parish court

Attorneys in Westmoreland, Jamaica, are demanding the government prioritize the restoration of the parish court, which has been operating in inadequate facilities since Hurricane Melissa. The court's displacement has caused a backlog of cases and imposed a burden on litigants, who must travel outside the parish at their own expense. This has compromised access to justice for the most vulnerable citizens.

This story frames Black people as deserving of access to justice and equal treatment under the law, highlighting the structural barriers that prevent them from receiving fair treatment. The narrative implies that the government has a responsibility to ensure that its citizens have access to functional and dignified court facilities.

Section of Lacovia to Holland Bamboo main road to close this weekend

A section of the Lacovia to Holland Bamboo main road in Jamaica will be closed on Sunday for emergency infrastructure works. The closure is necessary to replace a damaged pipe culvert, which has caused hazardous driving conditions. Motorists will be diverted through Cuffies Pen during the closure.
